But the economic stability of the *Estado Novo* regime was damaged by the worldwide inflation, which help cause the Carnation Revolution which toppled the *Estado Novo* regime in April 1974 and led to the restoration of democracy.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAccornero2016120-156) Portugal granted independence to all of its colonies except for Macau in 1975.

The British historian Rob Skinner wrote the embargo had a "significant impact" on the South African economy.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESkinner2017117-157) The inflationary rise in commodity prices increased the input costs of South African manufacturing, making South African manufactured goods uncompetitive on the world market.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESkinner2017117-157) The rise in the price of gold as a hedge against inflation (South Africa has the world's largest gold mines) caused the *rand* to increase in value, which caused further problems for South African manufacturing.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESkinner2017117-157)

The strategy pursued by the National Party government since 1948 of sponsoring the industrialization of South Africa was derailed by the oil shock, with what Skinner described as "important social and political consequences" for the *apartheid* regime.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESkinner2017117-157) The [Soweto uprising](/wiki/Soweto_uprising "Soweto uprising") of 1976 was at least in part caused by anger at the high unemployment rate in the black South African community.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESkinner2017117-157)

Even worse hit than South Africa was Rhodesia (modern Zimbabwe), where oil was already expensive as the [white supremacist](/wiki/White_supremacist "White supremacist") government of Rhodesia had been under United Nations sanctions since 1965, and thus the smugglers who sold Rhodesia oil charged a premium for their services.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHermeleOdén198818-158) The premium charged by the "sanctions busters" such as Iran increased after the embargo, which threw the Rhodesian economy into recession.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHermeleOdén198818-158) Despite the United Nations sanctions, Rhodesia was a major exporter of chrome, steel and tobacco with the United States exempting "strategical" Rhodesian products such as chrome from the sanctions.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHermeleOdén198817-159)

The global recession of 1973–1975 proved more effective than the United Nations in ending the global demand for Rhodesian products, which made the Rhodesian recession especially severe.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHermeleOdén198818-158) Unlike South Africa, which had a sizable and long-standing white population, the white population of Rhodesia was much smaller and more recent. Rhodesia depended considerably more than South Africa on white immigration to provide enough soldiers for its army, while most of the Rhodesian whites had only arrived in the 1950s and did not have very deep roots in the country.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHermeleOdén198819-160)

The white flight caused by the recession put the Rhodesian Army at a major disadvantage in its war against the black guerrillas. The war cost the Rhodesian government one million Rhodesian dollars per day by 1975, and the costs of the war threatened to bankrupt Rhodesia.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHermeleOdén198819-160) In 1979, Rhodesia signed the Lancaster House Agreement, which led to Black majority rule in 1980.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHermeleOdén198819-160)

### Price controls and rationing

#### United States

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/FLAG_POLICY_DURING_THE_1973_oil_crisis.gif/250px-FLAG_POLICY_DURING_THE_1973_oil_crisis.gif)](/wiki/File:FLAG_POLICY_DURING_THE_1973_oil_crisis.gif)

Oregon gasoline dealers displayed signs explaining the flag policy in the winter of 1973–74.

[Price controls](/wiki/Price_controls "Price controls") exacerbated the crisis in the US.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The system limited the price of "old oil", that which had already been discovered, while allowing newly discovered oil to be sold at a higher price, to encourage investment. As a result, old oil was withdrawn from the market, creating greater scarcity. The rule also discouraged development of [alternative energies](/wiki/Alternative_energy "Alternative energy").[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrum2000313-161) The rule had been intended to promote [oil exploration](/wiki/Oil_exploration "Oil exploration").[[162]](#cite_note-162)

Scarcity was addressed by rationing, as in many countries. Motorists faced long lines at gas stations beginning in summer 1972 and increasing by summer 1973.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrum2000313-161) A sign of the change caused by the crisis was that in 1974 the [Exxon](/wiki/ExxonMobil "ExxonMobil") oil company replaced the General Motors corporation to be the largest corporation in the world, as measured by gross sales revenues.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELacey1981404-39)

In 1973, Nixon named [William E. Simon](/wiki/William_E._Simon "William E. Simon") as the first Administrator of the Federal Energy Office, a short-term organization created to coordinate the response to the embargo.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrum2000312-163) Simon allocated states the same amount of domestic oil for 1974 that each had consumed in 1972, which worked for states whose populations were not increasing.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrum2000320-164) In other states, lines at gasoline stations were common. The [American Automobile Association](/wiki/American_Automobile_Association "American Automobile Association") reported that in the last week of February 1974, 20% of American gasoline stations had no fuel.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrum2000320-164)

[Odd–even rationing](/wiki/Odd%E2%80%93even_rationing "Odd–even rationing") allowed vehicles with [license plates](/wiki/License_plate "License plate") having an odd number as the last digit, or a [vanity license plate](/wiki/Vanity_plate "Vanity plate"), to buy gas only on odd-numbered days of the month. Others could buy only on even-numbered days.[[165]](#cite_note-165)

In some states, a three-color flag system was used to denote gasoline availability at service stations—green for unrationed availability, yellow for restricted/rationed sales, and red for out of stock.[[166]](#cite_note-166)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/U.S._gas_rationing_stamps_1974.jpg/250px-U.S._gas_rationing_stamps_1974.jpg)](/wiki/File:U.S._gas_rationing_stamps_1974.jpg)

Gasoline ration stamps printed by the [Bureau of Engraving and Printing](/wiki/Bureau_of_Engraving_and_Printing "Bureau of Engraving and Printing") in 1974, but not used

Rationing led to violent incidents, when truck drivers chose to strike for two days in December 1973 over the limited supplies that Simon had allocated for their industry. In [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") and [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), non-striking truckers were shot at by striking truckers. In [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas"), trucks of non-strikers were attacked with bombs.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrum2000320-164)

America had controlled the price of natural gas since the 1950s. With the inflation of the 1970s, the price was too low to encourage the search for new reserves.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrum2000321-167) America's natural gas reserves dwindled from 237 trillion in 1974 to 203 trillion[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] in 1978. The price controls were not changed, despite president [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford")'s repeated requests to Congress.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrum2000321-167)

### Conservation and reduction in demand

#### United States

To help reduce consumption, in 1974 a [national maximum speed limit](/wiki/National_Maximum_Speed_Law "National Maximum Speed Law") of 55 mph (89 km/h) was imposed through the [Emergency Highway Energy Conservation Act](/wiki/Emergency_Highway_Energy_Conservation_Act "Emergency Highway Energy Conservation Act"). Development of the [Strategic Petroleum Reserve](/wiki/Strategic_Petroleum_Reserve_(United_States) "Strategic Petroleum Reserve (United States)") began in 1975, and in 1977 the cabinet-level [Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy") was created, followed by the [National Energy Act](/wiki/National_Energy_Act "National Energy Act") of 1978.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

In November 1995, President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") signed the [National Highway Designation Act](/wiki/National_Highway_System_(United_States)#Legislation "National Highway System (United States)"), ending the federal 55 mph speed limit, which allowed states to restore their prior maximum speed limit.[[169]](#cite_note-169) Year-round daylight saving time was implemented from January 6, 1974, to October 27, 1975, with a break between October 27, 1974, and February 23, 1975, when the country observed standard time. Parents complained that it forced many children to travel to school before sunrise. The prior rules were restored in 1976.[[170]](#cite_note-170)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Potlatch_gas.jpg/250px-Potlatch_gas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Potlatch_gas.jpg)

Gas stations abandoned during the crisis were sometimes used for other purposes. This station at [Potlatch](/wiki/Potlatch,_Washington "Potlatch, Washington"), Washington, was turned into a [revival](/wiki/Christian_revival "Christian revival") hall.

The crisis prompted a call to conserve energy, most notably a campaign by the [Advertising Council](/wiki/Advertising_Council "Advertising Council") using the tagline "Don't Be Fuelish".[[171]](#cite_note-171) Many newspapers carried advertisements featuring cut-outs that could be attached to light switches, reading "Last Out, Lights Out: Don't Be Fuelish".[[172]](#cite_note-172)

Although not regulated by the new legislation, auto racing groups voluntarily began conserving. In 1974, [NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR") reduced all race distances by 10%;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the [24 Hours of Daytona](/wiki/24_Hours_of_Daytona "24 Hours of Daytona") and the [12 Hours of Sebring](/wiki/12_Hours_of_Sebring "12 Hours of Sebring") race were cancelled.[[173]](#cite_note-173)

In 1975, the [Energy Policy and Conservation Act](/wiki/Energy_Policy_and_Conservation_Act "Energy Policy and Conservation Act") was passed, leading to the creation of the [Corporate Average Fuel Economy](/wiki/Corporate_Average_Fuel_Economy "Corporate Average Fuel Economy") (CAFE) standards that required improved fuel economy for cars and light trucks.[[174]](#cite_note-174)

In 1976, Congress created the [Weatherization Assistance Program](/wiki/Weatherization_Assistance_Program "Weatherization Assistance Program") to help low-income homeowners and renters reduce their demand for heating and cooling through better [insulation](/wiki/Insulation_(for_buildings) "Insulation (for buildings)").[[175]](#cite_note-175)

By 1980, domestic luxury cars with a 130-inch (3.3 m) [wheelbase](/wiki/Wheelbase "Wheelbase") and gross weights averaging 4,500 pounds (2,041 kg) were no longer made.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The automakers had begun phasing out the traditional front engine/rear-wheel drive layout in compact cars in favor of lighter front engine/front-wheel drive designs. A higher percentage of cars offered more efficient four-cylinder engines. Domestic auto makers also began offering more fuel efficient diesel powered passenger cars as well.

### Alternative energy sources

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/WOMEN_USES_HER_HOME_FIREPLACE_FOR_HEAT._A_NEWSPAPER_HEADLINE_BEFORE_HER_TELLS_OF_THE_COMMUNITY%27S_LACK_OF_HEATING_OIL_-_NARA_-_555393.jpg/250px-WOMEN_USES_HER_HOME_FIREPLACE_FOR_HEAT._A_NEWSPAPER_HEADLINE_BEFORE_HER_TELLS_OF_THE_COMMUNITY%27S_LACK_OF_HEATING_OIL_-_NARA_-_555393.jpg)](/wiki/File:WOMEN_USES_HER_HOME_FIREPLACE_FOR_HEAT._A_NEWSPAPER_HEADLINE_BEFORE_HER_TELLS_OF_THE_COMMUNITY%27S_LACK_OF_HEATING_OIL_-_NARA_-_555393.jpg)

A woman in Oregon uses [wood](/wiki/Wood_fuel "Wood fuel") in a [fireplace](/wiki/Fireplace "Fireplace") for heat. An October 1973 newspaper headline before her tells of the community's lack of heating oil.

The energy crisis led to greater interest in renewable energy, nuclear power and domestic fossil fuels.[[176]](#cite_note-GT-DEX-1973-00-176) In 2013, [Peter Grossman](/wiki/Peter_Grossman "Peter Grossman") opined that American energy policies after the crisis became and remained dominated by crisis-mentality thinking, promoting expensive quick fixes and single-shot solutions that ignore market and technology realities. He wrote that instead of providing stable rules that support basic research while leaving plenty of scope for entrepreneurship and innovation, Congresses and presidents repeatedly backed policies which promised politically expedient solutions whose true prospects were doubtful.[[177]](#cite_note-177)

The Brazilian government implemented its [Proálcool](/wiki/Pro%C3%A1lcool "Proálcool") (pro-alcohol) project in 1975 that mixed [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol") with gasoline for automotive fuel.[[178]](#cite_note-Bastos-178)

Israel was one of the few countries unaffected by the embargo, since it could extract sufficient oil from the Sinai. But to supplement Israel's over-taxed power grid, [Harry Zvi Tabor](/wiki/Harry_Zvi_Tabor "Harry Zvi Tabor"), the father of [Israel's solar industry](/wiki/Solar_power_in_Israel "Solar power in Israel"), developed the prototype for a [solar water heater](/wiki/Solar_water_heater "Solar water heater") now used in over 90% of Israeli homes.[[179]](#cite_note-BW-179)

### Macroeconomy

Western central banks decided to sharply cut interest rates to encourage growth, deciding that inflation was a secondary concern. Although this was the orthodox macroeconomic prescription at the time, the resulting [stagflation](/wiki/Stagflation "Stagflation") surprised economists and central bankers. The policy is now considered by some to have deepened and lengthened the adverse effects of the embargo. Recent research claims that in the period after 1985 the economy became more resilient to energy price increases.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

The price shock created large current account deficits in oil-importing economies. A [petrodollar recycling](/wiki/Petrodollar_recycling "Petrodollar recycling") mechanism was created, through which OPEC surplus funds were channeled through the capital markets to the West to finance the current account deficits. The functioning of this mechanism required the relaxation of [capital controls](/wiki/Capital_controls "Capital controls") in oil-importing economies. It marked the beginning of an exponential growth of Western [capital markets](/wiki/Capital_markets "Capital markets").[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasouros201360–62-181)

In the United States in 1974, seven of the 15 top Fortune 500 companies were oil companies, falling to four in 2014.[[182]](#cite_note-182)

### International relations

The crisis had a major impact on international relations and created a rift within NATO. Some European nations and Japan sought to disassociate themselves from [United States foreign policy in the Middle East](/wiki/United_States_foreign_policy_in_the_Middle_East "United States foreign policy in the Middle East") to avoid being targeted by the boycott. Arab oil producers linked any future policy changes to peace between the belligerents. To address this, the Nixon Administration began multilateral negotiations with the combatants. They arranged for Israel to pull back from the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula") and the [Golan Heights](/wiki/Golan_Heights "Golan Heights"). By January 18, 1974, [US Secretary of State](/wiki/US_Secretary_of_State "US Secretary of State") [Henry Kissinger](/wiki/Henry_Kissinger "Henry Kissinger") had negotiated an Israeli troop withdrawal from parts of the Sinai Peninsula. The promise of a negotiated settlement between Israel and Syria was enough to convince Arab oil producers to lift the embargo in March 1974.[[2]](#cite_note-USstate2ndCrisis-2) and again during the [1979 energy crisis](/wiki/1979_energy_crisis "1979 energy crisis").

#### United States

America's Cold War policies suffered a major blow from the embargo. They had focused on China and the Soviet Union, but the latent challenge to US hegemony coming from the [Third World](/wiki/Third_World "Third World") became evident. In 2004, declassified documents revealed that the US was so distraught by the rise in oil prices and being challenged by underdeveloped countries that they briefly considered military action to forcibly seize Middle Eastern oilfields in late 1973.[[183]](#cite_note-183)

Although no explicit plan was mentioned, a conversation between [US Secretary of Defense](/wiki/U.S._Secretary_of_Defense "U.S. Secretary of Defense") [James Schlesinger](/wiki/James_Schlesinger "James Schlesinger") and [British Ambassador to the United States](/wiki/British_Ambassador_to_the_United_States "British Ambassador to the United States") [Lord Cromer](/wiki/Rowland_Baring,_3rd_Earl_of_Cromer "Rowland Baring, 3rd Earl of Cromer") revealed Schlesinger had told him that "it was no longer obvious to him that the US could not use force." British Prime Minister [Edward Heath](/wiki/Edward_Heath "Edward Heath") was so worried by this prospect that he ordered a British intelligence estimate of US intentions, which concluded that America "might consider it could not tolerate a situation in which the US and its allies were at the mercy of a small group of unreasonable countries", and that they would prefer a rapid operation to seize oilfields in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, and possibly Abu Dhabi if military action was decided upon. Although the Soviet response to such an act would likely not involve force, intelligence warned "the American occupation would need to last 10 years as the West developed alternative energy sources, and would result in the 'total alienation' of the Arabs and much of the rest of the Third World."[[184]](#cite_note-184)

#### NATO

Western Europe began switching from pro-Israel to more pro-Arab policies.[[185]](#cite_note-185)[[186]](#cite_note-186)[[187]](#cite_note-187) This change strained the Western alliance. The US, which imported only 12% of its oil from the Middle East (compared with 80% for the Europeans and over 90% for Japan), remained staunchly committed to Israel. The percentage of US oil which comes from the nations bordering the Persian Gulf remained steady over the decades, with a figure of a little more than 10% in 2008.[[188]](#cite_note-188)

With the embargo in place, many developed countries altered their policies regarding the Arab-Israeli conflict. These included the UK, which refused to allow the United States to use British bases and [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") to airlift resupplies to Israel, along with the rest of the members of the [European Community](/wiki/European_Community "European Community").[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELicklider1988216-189)

Canada shifted towards a more pro-Arab position after displeasure was expressed towards Canada's mostly neutral position. "On the other hand, after the embargo the Canadian government moved quickly indeed toward the Arab position, despite its low dependence on Middle Eastern oil".[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELicklider1988214-190)

#### Japan

Although lacking historical connections to the Middle East, Japan was the country most dependent on Arab oil. 71% of its imported oil came from the Middle East in 1970. On November 7, 1973, the Saudi and Kuwaiti governments declared Japan a "nonfriendly" country, to encourage it to change its noninvolvement policy. It received a 5% production cut in December, causing a panic. On November 22, Japan issued a statement "asserting that Israel should withdraw from all of the 1967 territories, advocating Palestinian self-determination, and threatening to reconsider its policy toward Israel if Israel refused to accept these preconditions".[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELicklider1988214-190) By December 25, Japan was considered an Arab-friendly state.

#### Nonaligned nations

The oil embargo was announced roughly one month after a [right-wing military coup](/wiki/1973_Chilean_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1973 Chilean coup d'état") in [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") led by General [Augusto Pinochet](/wiki/Augusto_Pinochet "Augusto Pinochet") toppled socialist president [Salvador Allende](/wiki/Salvador_Allende "Salvador Allende") on September 11, 1973. The response of the Nixon administration was to propose doubling arms sales. As a consequence, an opposing Latin American bloc was organized and financed in part by Venezuelan oil revenues, which quadrupled between 1970 and 1975.

A year after the start of the embargo, the UN's nonaligned bloc passed a resolution demanding the creation of a "[New International Economic Order](/wiki/New_International_Economic_Order "New International Economic Order")" under which nations within the global South would receive a greater share of benefits derived from the exploitation of southern resources and greater control over their self-development.[[191]](#cite_note-191)

#### Arab states

Prior to the embargo, the geo-political competition between the Soviet Union and the United States, in combination with low oil prices that hindered the necessity and feasibility of alternative energy sources, presented the Arab States with financial security, moderate economic growth, and disproportionate international bargaining power.[[192]](#cite_note-192)

The oil shock disrupted the status quo relationships between Arab countries and the US and USSR. At the time, Egypt, Syria and Iraq were allied with the USSR. Saudi Arabia, Turkey and Iran, plus Israel, aligned with the US. Vacillations in alignment often resulted in greater support from the respective superpowers. When [Anwar Sadat](/wiki/Anwar_Sadat "Anwar Sadat") became president of Egypt in 1970, he dismissed Soviet specialists in Egypt and reoriented towards the US.

Concerns over economic domination from increased Soviet oil production turned into fears of military aggression after the [1979 Soviet invasion of Afghanistan](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War"), a major turning point in Cold War geopolitics.[[193]](#cite_note-193) Persian Gulf states turned toward the US for security guarantees against Soviet military action, coming at a time marked by increased American weapons sales, technology, and outright military presence to various US-allied nations. Saudi Arabia and Iran became increasingly dependent on American security assurances to manage both external and internal threats, including increased military competition between them over increased oil revenues. Both states were competing for preeminence in the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf"), and using increased revenues to fund expanded militaries. By 1979, Saudi arms purchases from the US were five times larger than Israel's.[[194]](#cite_note-194)

In the wake of the 1979 [Iranian Revolution](/wiki/Iranian_Revolution "Iranian Revolution") the Saudis were forced to deal with the prospect of internal destabilization via the radicalism of [Islamism](/wiki/Islamism "Islamism"), a reality which was quickly revealed in the [Grand Mosque seizure](/wiki/Grand_Mosque_seizure "Grand Mosque seizure") in Mecca by [Wahhabi](/wiki/Wahhabi "Wahhabi") extremists in November 1979, and a Shiite Muslim revolt in the oil rich [Al-Hasa](/wiki/Al-Ahsa_Oasis "Al-Ahsa Oasis") region of Saudi Arabia in December of the same year, which was known as the [1979 Qatif Uprising](/wiki/1979_Qatif_Uprising "1979 Qatif Uprising").[[195]](#cite_note-195) [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") is a near-absolute monarchy, an Arabic speaking country, and has a [Sunni](/wiki/Sunni "Sunni") Muslim majority, while Persian speaking [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") since 1979 is an Islamist theocracy with a [Shiite](/wiki/Shiite "Shiite") Muslim majority, which explains the current hostility between Saudi Arabia and Iran.[[196]](#cite_note-196)

Before the Iranian Revolution the usually [pro-American](/wiki/Pro-American "Pro-American"), [anti-communist](/wiki/Anti-communist "Anti-communist") and largely Sunni Muslim Saudi Arabians were somewhat wary of the [pro-Soviet relations](/wiki/Iraq%E2%80%93Russia_relations "Iraq–Russia relations") held by the [Ba'athist](/wiki/Ba%27athism "Ba'athism") socialist and relatively secularist republican dictatorship of Iraq, the latter of which is a majority Shiite Muslim Arab nation which was ruled by a Sunni Muslim Arab minority before the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"), and how that affected the Saudis' own [relations with the Iraqis](/wiki/Iraq%E2%80%93Saudi_Arabia_relations "Iraq–Saudi Arabia relations"), because these two oil-rich Arab nations share a long land border with each other.[[197]](#cite_note-197)

### Automobile industry

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/1973_oil_crisis "Special:EditPage/1973 oil crisis") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(October 2017)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The oil crisis sent a signal to the auto industry globally, which changed many aspects of production and usage for decades to come.

#### Western Europe

After World War II, most West European countries taxed motor fuel to limit imports, and as a result most cars made in Europe were smaller and more economical than their American counterparts. By the late 1960s, increasing incomes supported rising car sizes.

The oil crisis pushed West European car buyers away from larger, less economical cars.[[198]](#cite_note-198) The most notable result of this transition was the rise in popularity of compact [hatchbacks](/wiki/Hatchback "Hatchback"). The only notable small hatchbacks built in Western Europe before the oil crisis were the [Peugeot 104](/wiki/Peugeot_104 "Peugeot 104"), [Renault 5](/wiki/Renault_5 "Renault 5") and [Fiat 127](/wiki/Fiat_127 "Fiat 127"). By the end of the decade, the market had expanded with the introduction of the [Ford Fiesta](/wiki/Ford_Fiesta "Ford Fiesta"), [Opel Kadett](/wiki/Opel_Kadett "Opel Kadett") (sold as the [Vauxhall Astra](/wiki/Vauxhall_Astra "Vauxhall Astra") in Great Britain), [Chrysler Sunbeam](/wiki/Chrysler_Sunbeam "Chrysler Sunbeam") and [Citroën Visa](/wiki/Citro%C3%ABn_Visa "Citroën Visa").

Buyers looking for larger cars were increasingly drawn to medium-sized hatchbacks. Virtually unknown in Europe in 1973, by the end of the decade they were gradually replacing [sedans](/wiki/Sedan_(automobile) "Sedan (automobile)") as the mainstay of this sector. Between 1973 and 1980, medium-sized hatchbacks were launched across Europe: the [Chrysler/Simca Horizon](/wiki/Chrysler_Horizon "Chrysler Horizon"), [Fiat Ritmo](/wiki/Fiat_Ritmo "Fiat Ritmo") (Strada in the UK), [Ford Escort MK3](/wiki/Ford_Escort_(Europe) "Ford Escort (Europe)"), [Renault 14](/wiki/Renault_14 "Renault 14"), [Volvo 340 / 360](/wiki/Volvo_300_Series "Volvo 300 Series"), [Opel Kadett](/wiki/Opel_Kadett "Opel Kadett"), and [Volkswagen Golf](/wiki/Volkswagen_Golf "Volkswagen Golf").

These cars were considerably more economical than the traditional [saloons](/wiki/Saloon_(car) "Saloon (car)") they were replacing, and attracted buyers who traditionally bought larger vehicles. Some 15 years after the oil crisis, hatchbacks dominated most European small and medium car markets, and had gained a substantial share of the large family car market.

#### United States

See also: [Malaise era](/wiki/Malaise_era "Malaise era")

See also: [Automotive industry in the United States](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_the_United_States "Automotive industry in the United States")

Before the energy crisis, large, heavy, and powerful cars were popular. By 1971, the standard engine in a [Chevrolet Caprice](/wiki/Chevrolet_Caprice#1969–1970 "Chevrolet Caprice") was a 400-cubic inch (6.5 liter) V8. The wheelbase of this car was 121.5 inches (3,090 mm), and *Motor Trend*'s 1972 road test of the similar [Chevrolet Impala](/wiki/Chevrolet_Impala "Chevrolet Impala") achieved no more than 15 highway miles per gallon. In the 15 years prior to the 1973 oil crisis, gasoline prices in the US had lagged well behind inflation.[[199]](#cite_note-gpcutinf-199)

The crisis reduced the demand for large cars.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrum2000321-167) Japanese imports, primarily the [Toyota Corona](/wiki/Toyota_Corona "Toyota Corona"), the [Toyota Corolla](/wiki/Toyota_Corolla "Toyota Corolla"), the [Datsun B210](/wiki/Datsun_B210 "Datsun B210"), the [Datsun 510](/wiki/Datsun_510 "Datsun 510"), the [Honda Civic](/wiki/Honda_Civic_(first_generation) "Honda Civic (first generation)"), the [Mitsubishi Galant](/wiki/Mitsubishi_Galant "Mitsubishi Galant") (a captive import from Chrysler sold as the [Dodge Colt](/wiki/Dodge_Colt "Dodge Colt")), the [Subaru DL](/wiki/Subaru_Leone "Subaru Leone"), and later the [Honda Accord](/wiki/Honda_Accord "Honda Accord") all had [four cylinder engines](/wiki/Straight_4 "Straight 4") that were more fuel efficient than the typical American [V8](/wiki/V8_engine "V8 engine") and [six cylinder engines](/wiki/Straight_6 "Straight 6"). Japanese imports became mass-market leaders with [unibody](/wiki/Unibody "Unibody") construction and front-wheel drive, which became [de facto standards](/wiki/De_facto_standard "De facto standard").

From Europe, the [Volkswagen Beetle](/wiki/Volkswagen_Beetle "Volkswagen Beetle"), the [Volkswagen Fastback](/wiki/Volkswagen_Type_3 "Volkswagen Type 3"), the [Renault 8](/wiki/Renault_8 "Renault 8"), the [Renault LeCar](/wiki/Renault_5 "Renault 5"), and the [Fiat Brava](/wiki/Fiat_131 "Fiat 131") were successful. Detroit responded with the [Ford Pinto](/wiki/Ford_Pinto "Ford Pinto"), the [Ford Maverick](/wiki/Ford_Maverick_(Americas) "Ford Maverick (Americas)"), the [Chevrolet Vega](/wiki/GM_H_platform_(RWD) "GM H platform (RWD)"), the [Chevrolet Nova](/wiki/GM_X_platform_(RWD) "GM X platform (RWD)"), the [Plymouth Valiant](/wiki/Chrysler_A_platform "Chrysler A platform") and the [Plymouth Volaré](/wiki/Chrysler_F_platform "Chrysler F platform"). American Motors sold its homegrown [Gremlin](/wiki/AMC_Gremlin "AMC Gremlin"), [Hornet](/wiki/AMC_Hornet "AMC Hornet") and [Pacer](/wiki/AMC_Pacer "AMC Pacer") models.

Some buyers lamented the small size of the first Japanese compacts, and both Toyota and Nissan (then known as Datsun) introduced larger cars such as the [Toyota Corona Mark II](/wiki/Toyota_Corona_Mark_II "Toyota Corona Mark II"), the [Toyota Cressida](/wiki/Toyota_Cressida "Toyota Cressida"), the [Mazda 616](/wiki/Mazda_Capella#616/618_(USA) "Mazda Capella") and [Datsun 810](/wiki/Datsun_810 "Datsun 810"), which added passenger space and amenities such as air conditioning, power steering, AM-FM radios, and even power windows and central locking without increasing the price of the vehicle. A decade after the 1973 oil crisis, Honda, Toyota and Nissan, affected by the 1981 [voluntary export restraints](/wiki/Voluntary_export_restraints "Voluntary export restraints"), opened US assembly plants and established their luxury divisions (Acura, Lexus and Infiniti, respectively) to distinguish themselves from their mass-market brands.

Compact trucks were introduced, such as the [Toyota Hilux](/wiki/Toyota_Hilux "Toyota Hilux") and the [Datsun Truck](/wiki/Datsun_Truck "Datsun Truck"), followed by the [Mazda Truck](/wiki/Mazda_B-Series_(North_America) "Mazda B-Series (North America)") (sold as the [Ford Courier](/wiki/Ford_Courier "Ford Courier")), and the Isuzu-built [Chevrolet LUV](/wiki/Chevrolet_LUV "Chevrolet LUV"). Mitsubishi rebranded its [Forte](/wiki/Mitsubishi_Triton "Mitsubishi Triton") as the [Dodge D-50](/wiki/Mitsubishi_Triton "Mitsubishi Triton") a few years after the oil crisis. Mazda, Mitsubishi and Isuzu had joint partnerships with Ford, Chrysler, and GM, respectively. Later, the American makers introduced their domestic replacements (Ford Ranger, Dodge Dakota and the Chevrolet S10/GMC S-15), ending their [captive import](/wiki/Captive_import "Captive import") policy.

An increase in imported cars into North America forced General Motors, Ford and Chrysler to introduce smaller and fuel-efficient models for domestic sales. The [Dodge Omni](/wiki/Dodge_Omni "Dodge Omni") / [Plymouth Horizon](/wiki/Plymouth_Horizon "Plymouth Horizon") from Chrysler, the [Ford Fiesta](/wiki/Ford_Fiesta "Ford Fiesta") and the [Chevrolet Chevette](/wiki/Chevrolet_Chevette "Chevrolet Chevette") all had four-cylinder engines and room for at least four passengers by the late 1970s. By 1985, the average American vehicle moved 17.4 miles per gallon, compared to 13.5 in 1970. The improvements stayed, even though the price of a barrel of oil remained constant at $12 from 1974 to 1979.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrum2000321-167)

Sales of large sedans for most makes, except [Chrysler](/wiki/Chrysler "Chrysler") products, recovered within two model years of the 1973 crisis. The [Cadillac DeVille](/wiki/Cadillac_DeVille "Cadillac DeVille") and [Fleetwood](/wiki/Cadillac_Fleetwood_Brougham "Cadillac Fleetwood Brougham"), [Buick Electra](/wiki/Buick_Electra "Buick Electra"), [Oldsmobile 98](/wiki/Oldsmobile_98 "Oldsmobile 98"), [Lincoln Continental](/wiki/Lincoln_Continental "Lincoln Continental"), [Mercury Marquis](/wiki/Mercury_Marquis "Mercury Marquis"), and various other luxury oriented sedans became popular again in the mid-1970s. The only full-size models that did not recover were lower price models such as the [Chevrolet Bel Air](/wiki/Chevrolet_Bel_Air "Chevrolet Bel Air") and [Ford Galaxie 500](/wiki/Ford_Galaxie_500 "Ford Galaxie 500"). Slightly smaller models such as the [Oldsmobile Cutlass](/wiki/Oldsmobile_Cutlass "Oldsmobile Cutlass"), [Chevrolet Monte Carlo](/wiki/Chevrolet_Monte_Carlo "Chevrolet Monte Carlo"), [Ford Thunderbird](/wiki/Ford_Thunderbird "Ford Thunderbird") and various others sold well.

Economical imports succeeded alongside heavy, expensive vehicles. In 1976, Toyota sold 346,920 cars (average weight around 2,100 lbs), while Cadillac sold 309,139 cars (average weight around 5,000 lbs).

Federal safety standards, such as NHTSA Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard 215 (pertaining to safety bumpers), and compacts like the 1974 Mustang I were a prelude to the DOT "downsize" revision of vehicle categories.[[200]](#cite_note-200) By 1977, GM's full-sized cars reflected the crisis.[[201]](#cite_note-Collectible_Automobile-201) By 1979, virtually all "full-size" American cars had shrunk, featuring smaller engines and smaller outside dimensions. [Chrysler](/wiki/Chrysler_Corporation "Chrysler Corporation") ended production of their full-sized luxury sedans at the end of the 1981 model year, moving instead to a full [front-wheel drive](/wiki/Front-wheel_drive "Front-wheel drive") lineup for 1982, except for the [M-body](/wiki/Chrysler_M_platform "Chrysler M platform") [Dodge Diplomat](/wiki/Dodge_Diplomat "Dodge Diplomat")/[Plymouth Gran Fury](/wiki/Plymouth_Gran_Fury "Plymouth Gran Fury") and [Chrysler New Yorker Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Chrysler_New_Yorker_Fifth_Avenue "Chrysler New Yorker Fifth Avenue") sedans.

## Consequences

### Decline of OPEC

Further information: [1980s oil glut](/wiki/1980s_oil_glut "1980s oil glut")

OPEC soon lost its preeminent position, and in 1981, its production was surpassed by that of other countries. Additionally, its own member nations were divided. Saudi Arabia, trying to recover market share, increased production, pushing prices down, shrinking or eliminating profits for high-cost producers. The world price, which had peaked during the [1979 energy crisis](/wiki/1979_energy_crisis "1979 energy crisis") at nearly $40 per barrel, decreased during the 1980s to less than $10 per barrel. Adjusted for inflation, oil briefly fell back to pre-1973 levels. This "sale" price was a windfall for oil-importing nations, both developing and developed.

During the [Iran–Iraq War](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_War "Iran–Iraq War") of 1980–1988, the stated war aim of Iran was to overthrow the Baath regime in Iraq.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBullochMorris1989163-202) As a consequence, Saudi Arabia and the other Arab Gulf states leaned in a very pro-Iraqi neutrality during the war.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBullochMorris1989163-202) As a part of its policy of supporting Iraq, Saudi Arabia pumped out oil in massive quantities to lower the price as a way of hurting Iran's economy.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrogan1990252-203)

The low price of oil also hurt Iraq's economy, which forced Iraq to borrow massive sums of money, putting Iraq deeply into debt. In contrast, Iran refused to borrow any money because of its refusal to pay interest on any loans, and paid the costs of the war directly from the sale of its oil.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrogan1990252-203)

Iran's revenue from the sale of oil went from $20 billion US dollars per year in 1982 to $5 billion US dollars per year by 1988, which pushed Iran to the verge of bankruptcy and forced Iran to make peace with Iraq later in 1988.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrogan1990252-203) During the war, the Iranian delegation at OPEC tried very hard to have the group cut production to raise prices, but were blocked by the other delegations led by the Saudi delegation, who insisted on more oil production.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrogan1990252-203)

### Oil sources diversification

The embargo encouraged new venues for energy exploration, including Alaska, the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"), the [Caspian Sea](/wiki/Caspian_Sea "Caspian Sea"), and the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus").[[204]](#cite_note-204) Exploration in the [Caspian Basin](/wiki/Caspian_Sea "Caspian Sea") and Siberia became profitable. Cooperation changed into a far more adversarial relationship as the USSR increased its production. By 1980, the Soviet Union had become the world's largest producer.[[205]](#cite_note-205)[[206]](#cite_note-206)

Part of the decline in prices and economic and geopolitical power of OPEC came from the move to alternative energy sources. OPEC had relied on [price inelasticity](/wiki/Price_elasticity_of_demand "Price elasticity of demand")[[207]](#cite_note-207) to maintain high consumption, but had underestimated the extent to which conservation and other sources of supply would eventually reduce demand. [Electricity generation](/wiki/Electricity_generation "Electricity generation") from [nuclear power](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power") and [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas"), home heating from natural gas, and [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol")-blended gasoline all reduced the demand for oil.

### Economic impact

The drop in prices presented a serious problem for oil-exporting countries in northern Europe and the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf"). Heavily populated, impoverished countries, whose economies were largely dependent on oil—including [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria"), and [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya")—did not prepare for a market reversal that left them in sometimes desperate situations.

When reduced demand and increased production glutted the world market in the mid-1980s, oil prices plummeted and the cartel lost its unity. Mexico (a non-member), Nigeria, and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"), whose economies had expanded in the 1970s, faced near-bankruptcy, and even Saudi Arabian economic power was significantly weakened. The divisions within OPEC made concerted action more difficult. As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=1973_oil_crisis&action=edit), OPEC has never approached its earlier dominance.
